title: Viva Ned Flanders

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Homer crashes car after nuclear plant dismissal
jokes.punchline: Iconic Homer exclamation of pain/frustration
jokes.explanation: Homer's signature catchphrase - perfect character integration and economy

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: I'm just thinking of my employees - All the cardsharps, bottom dealers and shills.
jokes.setup: Burns appears concerned about employees losing jobs
jokes.punchline: Reveals employees are all casino cheaters
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns subversion - fake compassion revealing corrupt nature

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I'm very concerned about my employees losing their jobs.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: But sir, they're all casino cheaters.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Oh, well then, fire them all!

jokes.text: They're managing your chain of nursing homes, sir.
jokes.setup: Burns worries about where crooked casino employees will go
jokes.punchline: They're already running nursing homes
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation - casino cheaters running nursing homes is brilliantly twisted

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Where will all these crooked casino employees go?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: They're already running nursing homes

jokes.text: Gone are such headliners as Little Timmy and the Shebangs... the Shebangs, and the New Shebangs, featuring Big Timmy.
jokes.setup: Listing casino entertainment acts
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous progression of similar band names with size reversal
jokes.explanation: Mildly amusing absurd band names but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Tonight's entertainment includes The Who, The What, The When, The Where, and The Why.

jokes.text: All right! Here comes the implosion. 'Implosion'? But I thought you said- [Crowd Clamoring]
jokes.setup: Homer announces implosion vs explosion
jokes.punchline: Building explodes outward instead of imploding
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection setup with visual payoff

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Today we witness the controlled implosion of the Springfield Nuclear Power Plant.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I thought you said it was going to implode?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It is! It's just... imploding outward.

jokes.text: Hockey puck! Don Rickles zinged you, Marge.
jokes.setup: Don Rickles makes insult in cartoon world
jokes.punchline: Homer recognizes classic Rickles insult style
jokes.explanation: Smart celebrity reference that works on multiple levels

dialogue.speaker: Don Rickles
dialogue.line: Why you dummy, you couldn't find your way out of a paper bag!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, that's the real Don Rickles!

jokes.text: I'm okay, but the Puerto Rican guy's trying to steal your hubcaps. Just kiddin; I'm a nice guy.
jokes.setup: Rickles appears to make racist comment after accident
jokes.punchline: Immediately reveals it was just his comedy persona
jokes.explanation: Clever commentary on comedian personas vs real personality

dialogue.speaker: Don Rickles
dialogue.line: What are you, some kinda...

dialogue.speaker: Don Rickles
dialogue.line: Just kidding! That's my act, baby. Don Rickles, the insult comic!

jokes.text: Aw, what's the rush? Might rain next week.
jokes.setup: Marge suggests washing dirty car
jokes.punchline: Homer's lazy logic about potential rain
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - lazy but delivered with such confidence

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, why don't you wash the car? It's filthy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wash it? But Marge, what if it rains tomorrow? Then it'll just get dirty again.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's not how that works.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, I'm not washing that car until I'm sure it won't rain for at least... a week.

jokes.text: You listen to your friends, but you never listen to me.
jokes.setup: Homer immediately goes to car wash when Ned suggests it
jokes.punchline: Marge points out the hypocrisy
jokes.explanation: Universal relationship dynamic, well-observed

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you just went to the car wash yesterday.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, but Ned said it was a good idea.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: So you'll listen to Ned but not to me? I've been telling you to wash the car for weeks!

jokes.text: Cover up your eyes and drop your britches. Who wants wax?
jokes.setup: Car wash worker treating children like cars
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate commands to kids in car wash
jokes.explanation: Absurd inappropriate treatment creating discomfort humor

dialogue.speaker: Car wash attendant
dialogue.line: Alright kids, line up! We're gonna get you all nice and shiny.

dialogue.speaker: Car wash attendant
dialogue.line: First one through, we're gonna spray you down real good.

dialogue.speaker: Car wash attendant
dialogue.line: Now hold still while I apply the undercarriage wash.

dialogue.speaker: Car wash attendant
dialogue.line: And finish it off with a nice hot wax!

jokes.text: No hablo ingles, senor.
jokes.setup: Ned carefully explains his valuable bumper sticker collection
jokes.punchline: Worker pretends not to understand English
jokes.explanation: Classic avoidance tactic, economical but predictable

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: This here's my prized collection of bumper stickers. I've got 'em organized by year and theme.

dialogue.speaker: Car wash worker
dialogue.line: ¿Qué?

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: No, no, I said bumper stickers. You know, the adhesive decals?

dialogue.speaker: Car wash worker
dialogue.line: No entiendo, señor.

jokes.text: Seals and Crofts? Pablo Cruise? Air Supply? Whoa, ho. Loggins and Oates. And it's free.
jokes.setup: Homer discovers free music collection
jokes.punchline: Gets excited about terrible soft rock bands
jokes.explanation: Good cultural reference humor - Homer's terrible taste in music

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Free music! Oh boy, oh boy!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let's see what we got here... Ah! The complete works of Air Supply!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And look - all of Bread's greatest hits! This is the best day of my life!

jokes.text: What kind of music do they play? Crap-Rock? No. Wuss-Rock? That's it.
jokes.setup: Lisa asks about the musical genre
jokes.punchline: Marge finds perfect insulting name for soft rock
jokes.explanation: Excellent coined term 'Wuss-Rock' - perfect description

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: What do you call that musical genre?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Soft rock.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But isn't that just an insult?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Yes, yes it is.

jokes.text: Hey, how did 'Churchy LaFemme' get half price?
jokes.setup: Homer sees Ned got discount
jokes.punchline: Mocking nickname for religious Ned
jokes.explanation: Creative nickname playing on Ned's religiosity

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey Ned, I saw you got a discount at the store. What'd you do, pray for a lower price?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: From now on I'm calling you 'Discount Diddly-Daddy'!

jokes.text: You see, 'Saint Flanders' is as crooked as you or me.
jokes.setup: Homer accuses perfect neighbor of fraud
jokes.punchline: Claims Ned is as corrupt as known troublemakers
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Homer's petty jealousy of Ned's goodness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Flanders is a fraud! He's just as corrupt as Barney, Lenny, and Carl!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, I don't know what you're talking about, Homer. I'm a God-fearing man.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure, sure. That's what they all say.

jokes.text: That's right. It's my sad duty to rat out this man for defrauding a car wash.
jokes.setup: Homer stands up in church with dramatic announcement
jokes.punchline: The 'crime' is getting a senior discount at a car wash
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer behavior - making a mountain out of a molehill with overdramatic presentation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I have a terrible confession to make. I committed a crime.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: What is it, Homer?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I got a senior discount at the car wash.

jokes.text: Now, I'm not a fancy, big-city lawyer - [All Gasp]
jokes.setup: Homer uses folksy legal phrase
jokes.punchline: Congregation gasps at the idea Homer might be a lawyer
jokes.explanation: Perfect reaction beat - absurd idea of Homer as lawyer

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I move to adjourn.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: All in favor?

dialogue.speaker: Congregation
dialogue.line: Aye.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Homer, you certainly know your parliamentary procedure.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I watch a lot of court TV.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Homer Simpson, a lawyer? That's the most ridiculous thing I ever heard!

dialogue.speaker: Congregation
dialogue.line: *gasps*

jokes.text: Aah! It burns!
jokes.setup: Someone suggests Ned's secret is holy water
jokes.punchline: Moe reacts as if holy water burns him like a demon
jokes.explanation: Classic Moe moment - implies he's so corrupt holy water burns him

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Holy water?! Get it away from me! Get it away!

jokes.text: You've never licked maple syrup off your lover's stomach?
jokes.setup: Series of questions about indulgences
jokes.punchline: Escalates to inappropriate sexual suggestion in church
jokes.explanation: Shock value in inappropriate setting, but lacks sophistication

jokes.text: Yeah, even the boy in the bubble had a deck of cards.
jokes.setup: Criticizing Ned for never living
jokes.punchline: Even extremely sheltered sick child had more fun
jokes.explanation: Brilliant comparison - extremely economical and cutting

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You never do anything fun, Ned. You're always working or praying or in some stupid clean-living thing.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, I enjoy plenty of wholesome activities, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Like what?

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Why, just last Sunday I reorganized my sock drawer by thread count.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ned, even that extremely sheltered sick kid had more fun than you.

jokes.text: Some people like chunky peanut butter, some like smooth. Mm-hmm, and some people just steer clear of that whole hornets' nest.
jokes.setup: Ned defends his cautious nature with metaphor
jokes.punchline: Extends metaphor absurdly to avoiding all choice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned characterization - overcautious to absurd degree

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, you see, life is like a box of chocolates - you never know what you're gonna get. So I just avoid the whole diddly-darn thing and eat plain oatmeal instead.

dialogue.speaker: Maude
dialogue.line: But Ned, surely you have to make some choices in life?

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: That's where you're wrong-diddly-rong, Maude! I've eliminated all choices. I wear the same outfit every day, eat the same meals, and I only speak in pre-approved sentences from the Good Book. Why, I've even hired a team of monks to make decisions for me, and I don't even trust them!

jokes.text: Am I that pre-diddly-ictable?
jokes.setup: Ned realizes his predictability
jokes.punchline: Uses his characteristic speech pattern while lamenting it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant self-aware joke - Ned unconsciously demonstrates his predictability

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I'll be diddly-doodly, I'm so predictable, I can't even surprise myself anymore!

jokes.text: Help! We're being carjacked. Don't get clever, old man. Now take us to Dress Barn.
jokes.setup: Dramatic carjacking scenario
jokes.punchline: Mundane destination request undermines danger
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection - threatening carjacking for shopping trip

dialogue.speaker: Carjacker
dialogue.line: This is a carjacking! Give me your car!

dialogue.speaker: Elderly Man
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, just don't hurt us!

dialogue.speaker: Carjacker
dialogue.line: Now drive! Where are we going?

dialogue.speaker: Elderly Woman
dialogue.line: Could you take us to the Shuffleboard tournament?

dialogue.speaker: Carjacker
dialogue.line: ...What?

jokes.text: Do I remind you of troubled troubadour David Crosby? No, you remind me of silly billy Ned Flanders.
jokes.setup: Ned tries to look cool and rebellious
jokes.punchline: Wife sees right through his mild rebellion
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Ned's failed attempt at rebellion

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: I'm going to do something crazy today, Maude. I'm going to wear my shirt... untucked!

dialogue.speaker: Maude
dialogue.line: Neddy, you're still wearing your pocket protector and your glasses are on a lanyard.

jokes.text: Would a silly billy sit like this? [Snap] Ooh! Rod, Call Dr. Stein.
jokes.setup: Ned tries to prove he's not predictable with a dramatic pose
jokes.punchline: He immediately injures himself and needs medical attention
jokes.explanation: Perfect physical comedy showing Ned's attempt at rebellion immediately backfiring

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: I'll show you I'm not predictable! Behold!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Ow! My back! Someone call an ambulance!

dialogue.speaker: Maude
dialogue.line: Oh Neddy, you're as predictable as ever.

jokes.text: Well, well, well. So Flawless Flanders needs help from Stinky Pants Simpson.
jokes.setup: Ned asks Homer for help having fun
jokes.punchline: Homer relishes the role reversal with silly nicknames
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer pettiness with characteristic self-aware nicknames

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Homer, I need your help. I want to learn how to have fun.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, well, well. The studious reversal of fortune! From now on, you're 'Fun-diddly-un' and I'm the 'Diddly-Daddy of Good Times.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: This is the greatest day of my life!

jokes.text: Wellity, wellity, wellity. Stop that!
jokes.setup: Homer keeps saying 'wellity' with increasing smugness
jokes.punchline: Ned finally snaps at the annoying repetition
jokes.explanation: Simple escalation gag - Homer being deliberately annoying

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wellity, wellity, wellity!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wellity, wellity, wellity, wellity!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wellity, wellity, wellity, wellity, wellity!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Homer, for the love of all that is holy, STOP saying wellity!

jokes.text: Oh, my stars. I can't do that.
jokes.setup: Homer asks Ned to sign power of attorney
jokes.punchline: Ned's extremely mild oath of shock
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned characterization - even his shock is wholesome

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, I'll be diddly-doodly!

jokes.text: You're livin' up here. You gotta live down here, in the impulse zone.
jokes.setup: Homer's life philosophy lesson
jokes.punchline: Reducing life to shopping psychology
jokes.explanation: Clever metaphor showing Homer's consumer-driven worldview

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, there are three ways to do things: the right way, the wrong way, and the Max Power way!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Isn't that just the wrong way?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, but faster!

jokes.text: You're livin' up here. You gotta live down here, in the impulse zone.
jokes.setup: Homer explains life philosophy to Ned
jokes.punchline: References store marketing psychology as life advice
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer philosophy - consumer psychology as wisdom

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You see, Neddy, life is like a supermarket. You go in with a list, but you leave with a cart full of stuff you didn't need and no idea how you're gonna pay for it.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, I always stick to my list, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's your problem right there. You're not letting the end-cap displays of life guide you to your true destiny.

jokes.text: Homer, they blew that up yesterday.
jokes.setup: Homer dramatically announces casino plan
jokes.punchline: Ned points out obvious flaw in plan
jokes.explanation: Good callback to opening, highlights Homer's poor planning

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm gonna build a casino right here in Springfield!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, that's the dumbest idea I've ever heard.

jokes.text: Thanks, buddy. [Snickers] Sucker.
jokes.setup: Ned thanks Homer for calling Maude
jokes.punchline: Homer reveals he didn't actually call
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer selfishness - doesn't help but enjoys Ned's trust

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Thank you for calling Maude.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I didn't actually call.

jokes.text: It's-It's all designed to inflame the senses. I'm overstimulated. I gotta get out of this town.
jokes.setup: Ned experiences Las Vegas for the first time
jokes.punchline: Ned is immediately overwhelmed by the mild stimulation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned characterization - finding basic Vegas stimuli overwhelming

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, I'll be! All these neon lights and... and slot machines! And the people just walking around in their... their casual attire! Good heavens, is that a woman in PANTS?!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: I need to sit down. My heart's racing something fierce. Do they have any... any non-alcoholic beverages? Perhaps some milk? Warm milk would be nice.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: And what's with all the ringing and dinging? It's awfully loud, diddly-doodly! Ned needs a quiet place to pray and recover from all this... this SIN!

jokes.text: The lights, the noise, the letter 'X.' It's all designed to inflame the senses.
jokes.setup: Ned overwhelmed by Vegas
jokes.punchline: Being upset by the letter X specifically
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned moment - being offended by something as innocent as a letter

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not a complainer, but this city offends me in every way. The slot machines, the card games, the way the women dress...

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: And don't even get me started on the letter X. There's too many of 'em! X-rated this, X-rated that. It's everywhere!

jokes.text: Help me! [Screams] My daughter's not talented. [Groans]
jokes.setup: Chaotic Vegas street scene
jokes.punchline: Parent's brutal honesty about their child's lack of talent
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about stage parent delusions, economically delivered

dialogue.speaker: Parent
dialogue.line: Our son has no talent whatsoever.

dialogue.speaker: Other Parent
dialogue.line: We're just here hoping someone will discover him and take him far, far away.

jokes.text: When I wanna laugh, I'll take Bob Saget, thank you very much.
jokes.setup: Homer says daredevil laughs at death
jokes.punchline: Ned prefers safe, wholesome comedian
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned characterization - prefers sanitized entertainment

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A daredevil laughs at death.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, I prefer a safe, wholesome comedian.

jokes.text: You mean the one with the red paint? Uh, yeah, paint.
jokes.setup: Homer asks about X marked spot
jokes.punchline: Implies the 'paint' is actually blood from previous accidents
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about dangerous stunt history, well-executed misdirection

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What's with all the red paint on the ground?

dialogue.speaker: Performer
dialogue.line: Oh, that's not paint. That's where the last guy stood.

jokes.text: Well, actually, there are several rockets mounted to the- Boring.
jokes.setup: Coordinator tries to explain critical safety information
jokes.punchline: Homer dismisses rocket safety as boring
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer characterization - finding actual danger information boring

dialogue.speaker: Stunt Coordinator
dialogue.line: Now, this is critical safety information about the rocket.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, safety schmafety. Just make it go boom!

jokes.text: Hey, Flanders, don't spill my beer.
jokes.setup: Homer is about to be jumped over by motorcycle
jokes.punchline: His only concern is protecting his beer
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer priorities - beer more important than life-threatening stunt

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My beer!

jokes.text: Oh, I think someone just had his first taste of bloodlust.
jokes.setup: Ned admits wanting to see Homer get hurt
jokes.punchline: Homer diagnoses Ned's awakening violent urges
jokes.explanation: Good character development - Ned discovering his dark side

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: I'll admit it, Homer. Part of me wants to see you get hurt.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, Neddy, I think what you're experiencing is a natural thing. It's called being a man.

jokes.text: Let's see. What's Marge's birthday? Barney's is July 15, same as Lassie's... so Marge must be 50- Oh, forget it.
jokes.setup: Homer tries to remember meaningful dates for gambling
jokes.punchline: Homer knows his friend's and a dog's birthday but gives up on his wife's
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy showing Homer's misplaced priorities and terrible husband qualities

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let's see... Barney's birthday is May 21st, Santa's Little Helper's is October 2nd... Marge's birthday is... uh...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Eh, I give up.

jokes.text: Seven, eh? [Man] Seven a winner. Way to go, Flanders. The Bible's finally pulling its weight.
jokes.setup: Ned reluctantly gives biblical number
jokes.punchline: Homer treats religion as gambling system
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - religion as gambling strategy, highly quotable

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose the Good Book does say 'cast thy bread upon the waters.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yes! And it also says 'the Lord helps those who help themselves' to the collection plate!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm putting twenty bucks on Deuteronomy in the third race.

jokes.text: [Man With Deep Voice] Keep Gaming. What? It means gambling. Keep gambling.
jokes.setup: Ned prays for guidance about gambling
jokes.punchline: Mysterious voice tells him to keep gambling with clarification
jokes.explanation: Absurd divine intervention encouraging vice, with helpful clarification

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Dear Lord, I need guidance. Should I keep gambling?

dialogue.speaker: God
dialogue.line: Yes, Ned, keep gambling.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Oh thank the Lord! I knew you'd answer my prayers!

dialogue.speaker: God
dialogue.line: Well, actually, that was me, the Devil.

jokes.text: How do you do it, Homer? How do you silence that little voice that says, 'Think.' You mean Lisa?
jokes.setup: Ned asks about silencing the voice of reason
jokes.punchline: Homer thinks Ned means his daughter Lisa literally
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection and character integration - Homer's literal interpretation reveals family dynamics

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: How do you silence the voice of reason?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You kick Lisa out of the house.

jokes.text: I'll have a Shirley- No, a virgin- No, make it a children's- Oh, what the heck. You only live once. Give me a white wine spritzer.
jokes.setup: Ned tries to order increasingly mild drinks
jokes.punchline: Settles on extremely mild alcoholic drink as rebellion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned characterization - even his wildest rebellion is pathetically tame

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: I'd like a drink, but nothing too strong. What do you have that's... mild?

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: How about a light beer?

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: No, no, that's still beer. Even lighter?

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: Um... a wine cooler?

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Still contains alcohol! Do you have anything that's just barely alcoholic?

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: Well, there's near beer...

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Perfect! I'll take it. And make it a double! Woohoo!

jokes.text: Well, of course. It- [Screams] Who are you? Who am I? I'm Mrs. Ned Flanders.
jokes.setup: Ned wakes up hungover
jokes.punchline: Discovers he married a stranger in Vegas
jokes.explanation: Classic Vegas marriage trope but works for character development

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Wh-where am I? What happened last night?

dialogue.speaker: Ginger
dialogue.line: Good morning, husband.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Husband?! Who in blazes are you?

dialogue.speaker: Ginger
dialogue.line: We got married in Vegas, sweetie. Don't you remember?

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Married?! In Vegas?! Diddily-doodily, what have I done?

jokes.text: Boy, you did have a lot to drink last night, 'Homeo.'
jokes.setup: Homer doesn't remember getting married
jokes.punchline: Vegas wife calls him romantic nickname he doesn't remember
jokes.explanation: Standard Vegas wedding aftermath humor, mildly amusing

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Vegas wife? I was married in Vegas?

dialogue.speaker: Ginger
dialogue.line: Of course, you silly goose! Don't you remember? You said I was the love of your life!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Silly goose? I don't remember that at all.

jokes.text: I okilly-dokilly, 'schmokilly' do.
jokes.setup: Wedding video showing Ned's vows
jokes.punchline: Ned uses his characteristic speech pattern while drunk
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character consistency - even drunk Ned maintains speech pattern

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: I, Nedward Flanders, do take thee, Maude, to be my lawful wedded wife. To have and to hold from this day forward, for better or for worse, for richer or for poorer, in sickness and in health, till death do us part. Okily-dokily.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: And I promise to love you with all my heart, you beautiful, God-fearing woman. We'll raise our children in the ways of the Lord, and I'll never, ever let you down. Not in a million years, you hear me? Not a million years!

jokes.text: The bad news, ladies, is we already have wives. Well, you can't be very happy with them if you married us.
jokes.setup: Homer explains they already have wives
jokes.punchline: Ginger's logical conclusion about their marriage satisfaction
jokes.explanation: Solid logical comedy with reasonable structure and pacing

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We already have wives.

dialogue.speaker: Ginger
dialogue.line: Then you must be very happy.

jokes.text: You two fellas are the nicest husbands we've ever had - And we're not giving you up without a fight.
jokes.setup: Vegas wives seem sweet and understanding
jokes.punchline: Implies they've had multiple husbands and are possessive
jokes.explanation: Good escalation revealing the wives' true nature

jokes.text: Geez. I've never seen anyone so whipped so fast. [Imitates Whipcrack]
jokes.setup: Ned immediately obeys new wife's commands
jokes.punchline: Homer mocks with whip sound effect
jokes.explanation: Standard hen-pecked husband humor with sound effect

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: *makes whip cracking sound* Whipsshhh!

jokes.text: Having two wives could have its advantages. Chop, chop, dig, dig.
jokes.setup: Homer considers the benefits of polygamy
jokes.punchline: Immediately shown doing manual labor while wife orders him around
jokes.explanation: Good immediate ironic reversal - Homer's fantasy quickly becomes nightmare

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, polygamy isn't so bad. I could have multiple wives to share the load.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, get back to work!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yes, dear.

jokes.text: Ooh! A friendly bee. [Screams] Ow. That sting hurts so much.
jokes.setup: Homer cheerfully greets bee
jokes.punchline: Immediately gets stung and complains
jokes.explanation: Basic physical comedy, predictable outcome

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hello, bee!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ow! Ow! Ow! Why you little!

jokes.text: Blame me if you must, but don't ever speak ill of the program. The program is rock solid. The program is sound.
jokes.setup: Ned blames Homer's program for their situation
jokes.punchline: Homer defends his made-up program with cult-like devotion
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Homer's inability to accept responsibility, treating his advice as infallible

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: This is all your fault, Homer! Your program got us into this mess!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My program? My program is perfect! You just don't understand its beauty!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: But Homer, it's completely made up!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's what they said about religion, Flanders. Now shut your mouth before you offend the program!

jokes.text: Why did I bet the company payroll? Oh, Gil's in a lot of trouble.
jokes.setup: Gil loses at craps
jokes.punchline: Reveals he gambled with company money
jokes.explanation: Classic Gil characterization - always making bad decisions

dialogue.speaker: Gil
dialogue.line: Snake eyes! I'm cleaned out! Wait... I can cover this. I'll just borrow from the company till.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Gil, that's embezzlement!

dialogue.speaker: Gil
dialogue.line: I know, I know. But what choice do I have? I'm already a loser at craps!

jokes.text: Cold-hearted Homer, ditching his wife while ancient Ned runs for his life. Chips of red and blue and white, but we decide which-
jokes.setup: Chase scene in casino
jokes.punchline: Moody Blues narrate the action in their typical style
jokes.explanation: Creative celebrity cameo - band narrates action in their style

jokes.text: Well, I only saw them from the back 'cause they were so busy gang-probing you.
jokes.setup: Homer describes alien encounter to explain absence
jokes.punchline: Crude detail about Ned's supposed alien experience
jokes.explanation: Crude humor that relies on shock value rather than cleverness

jokes.text: Well, I only saw them from the back... 'cause they were so busy gang-probing you. Not the eyes! My eyes! Cover your eyes!
jokes.setup: Homer continues alien abduction lie
jokes.punchline: Birds attack their eyes, interrupting the ridiculous story
jokes.explanation: Random physical comedy that provides escape from the awkward situation but lacks craft

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So then the aliens took me to their leader, who was actually my third cousin...

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Homer, that's the most preposterous thing I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Ned, it all happened! And then they showed me the secret to—

dialogue.speaker: Birds
dialogue.line: *attack their eyes*

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ow! Ow! Get 'em off!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Good heavens!

tmdb_overview: When clean-living Ned Flanders wants to experience a new side of life, Homer takes him to Las Vegas. After a drunken binge, they marry a pair of cocktail waitresses.
insight: Ned's Vegas breakdown delivers 58 jokes at steady 2.52/min, but lacks standout peaks.
